title: M1 — Crab Nebula
text: M1, the Crab Nebula, is the remnant of a supernova witnessed by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD. Magnitude 8.4, in Taurus, distance 6,500 light-years. Best viewed November–February. In a 6-inch scope at 80x it appears as a faint oval smudge near zeta Tauri. An O-III filter helps. The Crab Pulsar at the center spins 30 times per second.

title: M13 — Hercules Globular Cluster
text: M13 is the brightest globular cluster in the northern hemisphere, magnitude 5.8, in Hercules. Best viewed May–September. At 150x in an 8-inch telescope you will resolve hundreds of individual stars across the core. About 145 light-years across at 22,200 light-years away. Naked-eye visible from dark sites as a fuzzy star.

title: M22 — Sagittarius Globular
text: M22 in Sagittarius is one of the four brightest globular clusters in the entire sky, magnitude 5.1. Best viewed June–September. Closer than M13 at 10,400 light-years. Resolves into thousands of stars in a 10-inch scope. Sits low for northern observers — best from latitudes south of 40°N.

title: M27 — Dumbbell Nebula
text: M27, the Dumbbell Nebula, is the brightest planetary nebula in the sky at magnitude 7.5, in Vulpecula. Distance 1,360 light-years. Best viewed June–October. Visible in binoculars as a faint patch. In a 6-inch at 80x with an O-III filter the apple-core shape is obvious. The central white dwarf is magnitude 13.8.

title: M31 — Andromeda Galaxy
text: M31, the Andromeda Galaxy, is the nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way at 2.5 million light-years. Magnitude 3.4, visible to the naked eye from dark skies as a faint smudge in Andromeda. Best viewed September–January from the northern hemisphere. In an 8-inch telescope at 50x, look for the bright nucleus and the dust lane on the southwest side. Companions M32 and M110 sit nearby and fit in the same low-power field.

title: M42 — Orion Nebula
text: M42, the Orion Nebula, is the closest large star-forming region at 1,344 light-years. Magnitude 4.0, visible naked-eye as the middle 'star' of Orion's sword. Best viewed November–March. At 100x in a 6-inch scope you will see the trapezium (four central young stars) and wispy nebulosity. A UHC or O-III filter dramatically increases contrast on the nebulosity. The whole complex extends across 24 light-years.

title: M44 — Beehive Cluster
text: M44, the Beehive (Praesepe), is a nearby open cluster in Cancer, magnitude 3.7, distance 577 light-years. Naked-eye visible as a fuzzy patch between Castor/Pollux and Regulus. Best viewed January–April. Use binoculars or a wide-field eyepiece — high magnification breaks up the cluster.

title: M45 — Pleiades
text: M45, the Pleiades or Seven Sisters, is a young open cluster at 444 light-years, magnitude 1.6. Best viewed October–March. Use binoculars or a wide-field eyepiece — at high magnification you only see a few stars. Faint blue reflection nebulosity appears in long-exposure photos but is difficult visually. Six to nine stars are visible to the naked eye depending on sky quality.

title: M51 — Whirlpool Galaxy
text: M51, the Whirlpool Galaxy, is a face-on spiral interacting with companion NGC 5195, in Canes Venatici near the Big Dipper handle. Magnitude 8.4, distance 31 million light-years. Best viewed March–July. Spiral arms become visible from dark sites in 10-inch and larger scopes; in smaller scopes you see two fuzzy patches.

title: M57 — Ring Nebula
text: M57, the Ring Nebula in Lyra, is the most famous planetary nebula. Magnitude 8.8, distance 2,300 light-years. Best viewed June–October. At 100x in a 4-inch you can see the tiny smoke-ring shape between Beta and Gamma Lyrae. A 10-inch scope at 200x shows clear ring structure. The 15th-magnitude central white dwarf needs a 14-inch or larger.

title: M81 + M82 — Galaxy Pair in Ursa Major
text: M81 (Bode's Galaxy, magnitude 6.9) and M82 (Cigar Galaxy, magnitude 8.4) form a famous pair in Ursa Major at 12 million light-years — both fit in the same low-power eyepiece field. Best viewed January–June. M81 is a clean spiral; M82 is an edge-on starburst with a dramatic dust lane visible in 8-inch+ scopes.

title: M101 — Pinwheel Galaxy
text: M101, the Pinwheel Galaxy, is a face-on spiral in Ursa Major near the handle of the Big Dipper. Magnitude 7.9 but the brightness is spread over a huge 28-arcminute disk, so it looks faint and easily lost in mediocre skies. Best viewed February–July. Needs dark skies and an 8-inch+ scope to see arm structure.

title: M104 — Sombrero Galaxy
text: M104, the Sombrero Galaxy in Virgo, is a near-edge-on spiral with a striking dark dust lane. Magnitude 8.0, distance 30 million light-years. Best viewed March–June. In a 6-inch you'll see the bright bulge; the dust lane crossing it becomes obvious in 10-inch+ scopes from dark sites.

title: M8 — Lagoon Nebula
text: M8, the Lagoon Nebula in Sagittarius, is one of the brightest emission nebulae. Magnitude 6.0, naked-eye visible from dark sites. Best viewed June–September. Combine the embedded open cluster NGC 6530 with the surrounding pink hydrogen-alpha glow. UHC filter makes it dramatic in any scope.

title: M16 Eagle + M17 Omega — Sagittarius Nebulae
text: M16, the Eagle Nebula (magnitude 6.0), contains the famous 'Pillars of Creation' photographed by Hubble. M17, the Omega/Swan Nebula (magnitude 6.0), sits 2.5° south. Both in Sagittarius/Serpens, best June–September. Use a UHC filter — M17 in particular shows a striking swan shape in a 6-inch.

title: M11 — Wild Duck Cluster
text: M11, the Wild Duck Cluster in Scutum, is the densest open cluster in the Messier catalog. Magnitude 5.8, distance 6,200 light-years. Best viewed June–September. At 80x it looks like a flock of birds in V-formation, with hundreds of stars packed tight.

title: M3 — Globular Cluster in Canes Venatici
text: M3 is one of the most beautiful globular clusters, magnitude 6.2, distance 33,900 light-years. Best viewed March–July. Resolves into individual stars at 100x in a 6-inch. Half a million stars packed into a 220-light-year sphere.

title: M5 — Globular Cluster in Serpens
text: M5 is one of the oldest known globular clusters, ~13 billion years old, magnitude 5.7, distance 24,500 light-years. Best viewed May–August. Slightly elongated rather than perfectly round. Resolves to the core in 8-inch scopes at 200x.

title: M35, M36, M37, M38 — Auriga Open Clusters
text: Four bright open clusters scattered across Auriga and Gemini, all magnitudes 5–7, best viewed November–March. M35 in Gemini is the brightest with hundreds of stars. M37 in Auriga is the richest. All are easy targets in binoculars; spectacular at low power in any scope.

title: M20 — Trifid Nebula
text: M20, the Trifid Nebula in Sagittarius, combines an emission nebula (red) with a reflection nebula (blue) split by dust lanes into three sections — hence 'trifid'. Magnitude 6.3, best June–September. The three-way dust split is visible in 8-inch scopes; UHC filter recommended.

title: M67 — One of the Oldest Open Clusters
text: M67 in Cancer is one of the oldest open clusters known at ~4 billion years (most open clusters dissipate before reaching 1 billion). Magnitude 6.1, best viewed January–April. Used as a calibration target by professional astronomers because its stellar population resembles the Sun's age.

title: Observing the Moon
text: The terminator (shadow line between lit and dark sides) is where detail is sharpest. Don't observe the full moon at high magnification — surface detail vanishes in the flat lighting. Best craters and mountains visible 3 days after new moon and a few days after full moon. A neutral density or polarizing filter cuts the glare on a near-full moon. Tycho, Copernicus, and the Apennine Mountains are the classic landmarks.

title: Observing Saturn
text: Saturn is the showpiece planet. At 100x you see the rings clearly. The Cassini Division (gap in the rings) becomes visible at 150x in steady seeing. Look for the shadow of the planet on the rings, and the shadow of the rings on the planet. Titan, the largest moon, is visible in any small telescope as a magnitude-8 point. Best viewed when at least 30 degrees above the horizon to escape atmospheric distortion.

title: Observing Jupiter
text: Jupiter shows surface detail at any aperture. At 80x you will see the two main equatorial belts and the four Galilean moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto). At 150x in steady seeing the Great Red Spot is visible if it's facing Earth — check a transit prediction app first. Moons cast shadows on the planet during transits, visible at 150x+ as black dots. The belts shift over months and years.

title: Observing Mars
text: Mars rewards only around opposition (every ~26 months) when it appears 15–25 arcseconds wide. At 200x in steady seeing during opposition, you can see the polar ice caps, dark albedo features (Syrtis Major is the easiest), and dust storm activity. Outside of opposition, Mars looks like a tiny orange dot with no detail. Use a yellow or red filter to enhance contrast on surface features.

title: Observing Venus
text: Venus is the brightest planet, often visible in daylight if you know where to look. Shows phases like the Moon — crescent when close to Earth, full on the far side of the Sun. No surface detail visible (cloud-shrouded). Best observed in twilight to reduce glare. A violet filter sometimes shows faint cloud structure.

title: Observing Mercury
text: Mercury is the hardest naked-eye planet because it never strays more than 28° from the Sun. Visible only in deep twilight, low on the horizon at greatest elongation (check an astronomy almanac). Shows phases in a telescope but no surface detail — atmospheric turbulence at low altitude smears everything.

title: Observing Uranus and Neptune
text: Uranus is naked-eye visible from dark sites at magnitude 5.7 but you need a finder chart — it looks like a star. In a telescope at 200x it's a tiny pale-cyan disk, ~3.5 arcseconds wide. Neptune is magnitude 7.8 and shows as a slightly larger blue dot at 250x. Neither shows surface detail in amateur scopes; finding them is the achievement.

title: Spotting the ISS and Bright Satellites
text: The International Space Station is brighter than any star (magnitude -3 at peaks) and crosses the sky in 5–7 minutes when it passes overhead. Use https://spotthestation.nasa.gov for predictions. In a tracking telescope at 200x you can see the solar panels and module structure. Iridium flares are gone (old sats deorbited), but Starlink trains are now common after launches.

title: Averted Vision
text: The center of your retina (fovea) has fewer rod cells, so faint deep-sky objects vanish when you stare directly at them. Look slightly to the side — about 10–15° — and the object becomes brighter. Practice on a faint galaxy: stare directly, then look beside it. The difference is dramatic. This is the single most important visual technique in deep-sky observing.

title: Dark Adaptation
text: Your eyes need 20–30 minutes in full darkness to reach maximum sensitivity to faint light. White light (phone screen, flashlight) destroys dark adaptation in seconds. Use a red flashlight or a phone screen with a deep red filter. Faint deep-sky objects (galaxies, nebulae) are dramatically more visible after full dark adaptation. Avoid looking at car headlights or your house porch light.

title: Seeing vs Transparency
text: Seeing is the steadiness of the air — affects how much detail you see on planets and the Moon. Good seeing means crisp views at 200x+. Transparency is how clear the sky is — affects how faint a deep-sky object you can see. A hazy night with steady air is good for planets but bad for galaxies. A crystal-clear windy night is the opposite. Both are rated 1–10 on most forecast sites.

title: Bortle Scale and Light Pollution
text: The Bortle scale ranks night-sky darkness from 1 (pristine, Milky Way casts shadows) to 9 (inner-city, only Moon and bright planets). Class 4 is 'rural/suburban transition' — Milky Way visible but washed out near horizon. Class 6 suburban — galaxies become hard. Class 8+ inner city — most deep-sky impossible. Tools like lightpollutionmap.info show your zone. Drive 1–2 hours from city to drop 2–3 Bortle classes.

title: UHC and O-III Filters for Nebulae
text: UHC (Ultra High Contrast) filters block most light pollution while passing the wavelengths emitted by hydrogen and oxygen in nebulae. They make a huge difference on emission nebulae like M42, M8, M16, M17. O-III filters are narrower — best on planetary nebulae and supernova remnants like M27, M57, M1. Filters do nothing for galaxies or star clusters. Don't waste money on cheap filters — Lumicon, Astronomik, or Optolong are the trusted brands.

title: Lunar and Polarizing Filters
text: A near-full Moon at high power is dazzling and reveals less detail than a half-Moon. A neutral-density (ND) lunar filter cuts brightness by 50–95%. A variable polarizing filter (two stacked polarizers) lets you dial brightness up and down. Both also cut glare on Venus and Jupiter when atmospheric seeing is poor.

title: Star Hopping
text: Star hopping is finding faint objects by chaining short jumps from bright stars you know to fainter ones, using a finder chart. Example: to find M51, start at Alkaid (the end of the Big Dipper handle), drop ~3.5° southwest to a magnitude-5 star, then ~1° west to the galaxy. Faster than GoTo for nearby objects, and builds real sky knowledge. Use a Telrad or red-dot finder for the bright stars, then a low-power eyepiece for the fine hop.

title: Ursa Major — The Great Bear
text: Ursa Major contains the Big Dipper asterism. The Pointer Stars (Dubhe, Merak) point to Polaris. Mizar in the handle is a famous double star — its companion Alcor is naked-eye visible. Hosts M81/M82 galaxy pair, M101 Pinwheel Galaxy, and M97 Owl Nebula. Circumpolar from northern temperate latitudes — visible every clear night.

title: Orion — The Hunter
text: Orion is the most recognizable winter constellation. Three belt stars (Alnitak, Alnilam, Mintaka) point south to Sirius. The sword below the belt contains M42, the Orion Nebula. Betelgeuse (red supergiant, top-left) and Rigel (blue supergiant, bottom-right) are the two brightest stars. Best viewed November–March. Contains M78, the Horsehead Nebula (very faint), and the Flame Nebula.

title: Cassiopeia — The Queen
text: Cassiopeia is the W-shaped circumpolar constellation opposite the Big Dipper across Polaris. Always visible from northern latitudes. Hosts open clusters M52, M103, NGC 457 (the Owl Cluster), and the Heart and Soul Nebulae. When the Big Dipper is low, Cassiopeia is high, and vice versa.

title: Lyra — The Lyre
text: Lyra is dominated by Vega, the 5th-brightest star, and forms part of the Summer Triangle (with Deneb in Cygnus and Altair in Aquila). Hosts M57 Ring Nebula, the famous Double Double (Epsilon Lyrae) — a quadruple star system that splits into two pairs at 200x — and M56 globular cluster. Best viewed June–October.

title: Cygnus — The Swan
text: Cygnus straddles the Milky Way and is rich in clusters and nebulae. Albireo at the swan's beak is the showcase double star — a stunning blue-and-gold contrast pair, visible at 50x in any scope. Contains the North America Nebula (NGC 7000), Veil Nebula supernova remnant, and many open clusters. Best viewed June–November.

title: Sagittarius — The Archer
text: Sagittarius points toward the center of the Milky Way and is the densest deep-sky region of the sky. Contains M8 Lagoon, M17 Omega, M20 Trifid, M22 globular, M55 globular, M28 globular, plus the Galactic Center itself. The 'teapot' asterism is the easiest pattern to find. Best viewed June–September from temperate northern latitudes (low on the southern horizon).

title: Scorpius — The Scorpion
text: Scorpius is one of the few constellations that actually looks like its name — a clear scorpion shape with curved tail and Antares (red supergiant) as the heart. Contains M4 globular cluster (one of the closest, 7,200 light-years), M6 Butterfly Cluster, M7 Ptolemy Cluster. Sits very low for northern observers — best from latitudes south of 35°N.

title: Leo — The Lion
text: Leo's backwards-question-mark asterism (the Sickle, with Regulus as the dot) is one of the easiest spring patterns. Hosts the Leo Triplet — M65, M66, NGC 3628 — three galaxies in the same low-power eyepiece field, plus M95, M96, M105. Best viewed February–June.

title: Andromeda — The Princess
text: Andromeda is the constellation hosting M31, the Andromeda Galaxy, plus its companions M32 and M110. The Great Square of Pegasus borders it — start at Alpheratz (the corner shared with Pegasus) and hop two stars north, then two stars west to find M31. Best viewed September–January.

title: Pegasus — The Winged Horse
text: Pegasus's defining feature is the Great Square — four stars forming a near-perfect square, visible from suburban skies. Borders Andromeda. Contains M15, one of the densest globular clusters. The constellation is autumn's anchor for star-hopping. Best viewed September–December.

title: Hercules — The Strongman
text: Hercules is a large but faint constellation between Lyra and Boötes. The Keystone asterism (four stars in a trapezoid) marks the body. M13, the brightest globular cluster in the northern hemisphere, sits on the western edge of the Keystone. M92, another fine globular, is in the head. Best viewed May–September.

title: Auriga — The Charioteer
text: Auriga is the bright pentagon of stars high in winter skies, anchored by Capella (6th-brightest star). Hosts three Messier open clusters — M36, M37, M38 — all easy in binoculars. Best viewed November–March.

title: Collimating a Reflector
text: A Newtonian reflector needs collimation (mirror alignment) every few sessions, or after transport. Use a laser collimator or Cheshire eyepiece. Adjust the secondary mirror first to center the primary, then adjust the primary to center the secondary's reflection. A poorly collimated scope shows soft, comet-shaped stars at high power. Star test by defocusing slightly on a bright star — concentric rings indicate good collimation, distorted rings mean misalignment.

title: Choosing Eyepieces
text: Magnification = telescope focal length / eyepiece focal length. A 1200mm scope with a 25mm eyepiece gives 48x. Useful magnification range is roughly 30x to 50x per inch of aperture — beyond that, the image gets dim and atmospheric seeing limits detail. A good starting set: 25mm or 32mm wide-field for finding objects, 10mm for medium power, 6mm for planetary detail, plus a 2x Barlow. Wider apparent field (68°+) gives more immersive views than narrow Plossls (50°).

title: Letting a Telescope Cool Down
text: A warm telescope brought outside into cold air shows poor image quality until the optics reach ambient temperature. Allow 30–60 minutes for a 6-inch scope, longer for larger apertures. Heat currents inside the tube cause shimmering even in steady seeing. Some Dobsonians have fans on the back of the primary mirror that speed cool-down to ~15 minutes.

title: Refractor vs Reflector vs Cassegrain
text: Refractors use lenses — sharp, low-maintenance, no collimation, but expensive per inch of aperture. Reflectors (Newtonians, Dobsonians) use mirrors — most aperture per dollar, but need collimation and eyepiece sits at the top. Schmidt-Cassegrains and Maksutovs use both — compact tube, GoTo-ready, but more expensive than Newtonians and slower to cool down. For visual deep-sky on a budget: 8" or 10" Dobsonian. For planetary at any budget: a quality apochromatic refractor.

title: Focal Ratio (f/Number)
text: Focal ratio = focal length / aperture. f/4 to f/6 is 'fast' — wide field, good for deep-sky and astrophotography, but more sensitive to eyepiece quality. f/8 to f/15 is 'slow' — narrower field, more forgiving on eyepieces, traditionally better for planets. A fast scope demands well-corrected eyepieces (Naglers, Ethos, Morpheus). A slow scope works fine with cheap Plossls.

title: GoTo vs Manual Mounts
text: GoTo mounts find objects by typing or selecting from a database — great for short observing sessions, light-polluted skies, or for beginners. Downsides: power needed, alignment routine, and you don't learn the sky. Manual Dobsonians are 'point and look' — fastest to set up, no batteries, you build real sky knowledge. For tracking planets at high power, GoTo or equatorial drives become essential.

title: Polar Alignment
text: An equatorial mount must be aligned to the celestial pole (Polaris in the north) so that one motor axis tracks the sky's east-west motion. Rough alignment: aim the mount's polar axis at Polaris using the latitude scale set to your latitude. Precise alignment (needed for astrophotography): use the polar scope reticle, or drift-alignment over 15–30 minutes. Visual observers can usually skip precise alignment.

title: Preventing Dew on Optics
text: Dew condenses on telescope optics on humid nights, especially refractors and SCTs (Newtonians are less affected because the primary is recessed). Fixes, in order: a dew shield (long lens hood) buys 1–2 hours; a dew heater strap (12V, low wattage) keeps optics 1–2°C above ambient indefinitely. Check the corrector plate regularly. A handheld 12V hairdryer on low can clear dew quickly without harming coatings.

title: Maximum Useful Magnification
text: Maximum useful magnification is roughly 50x per inch of aperture in steady seeing — so a 6-inch scope tops out around 300x, an 8-inch around 400x. Beyond that, the image is just dimmer and softer with no new detail. On most nights, atmospheric seeing limits useful magnification to ~150–200x regardless of aperture. The sweet spot for most deep-sky is 50x–100x.

title: Cleaning Telescope Optics
text: Don't. Optical coatings tolerate a surprising amount of dust before image quality suffers — typically years of normal use. Cleaning risks scratches and coating damage. When you must: blow dust with a rocket blower first, never wipe dry. For oily fingerprints, use Baader Wonder Fluid or a 50/50 distilled water + isopropyl alcohol mix on a clean lens tissue, one wipe per tissue, no pressure. Mirrors only every 2–3 years. Eyepieces more often (lash and breath condensation accumulate).

title: Best First Telescope
text: For a complete beginner, an 8-inch Dobsonian (e.g. Sky-Watcher Classic 200P, Orion XT8, Astroman Astrolab 200) is the standard recommendation: most aperture per dollar, fast setup (~2 minutes), no batteries, no alignment routine. For grab-and-go and travel: a 70–80mm refractor on an alt-az mount. Avoid: small department-store reflectors with shaky tripods (everything wobbles, magnification claims are nonsense), cheap GoTo scopes under $400 (mounts can't track reliably).

title: Astroman — Georgia's Astronomy Store
text: Astroman (astroman.ge) is Georgia's first dedicated astronomy retailer, based in Tbilisi. Stocks telescopes, mounts, eyepieces, filters, binoculars, and astrophotography gear from Sky-Watcher, Celestron, Bresser, Lacerta, ZWO, and others. Run by Revaz Modebadze. Physical showroom in Tbilisi for hands-on browsing and beginner consultation. Ships across Georgia and to neighboring countries.

title: Best Observing Sites in Georgia
text: Tbilisi is Bortle 7 (heavy light pollution). The Caucasus mountains 1.5–3 hours from Tbilisi reach Bortle 2–3 — among the darkest skies in Eastern Europe. Stepantsminda (Kazbegi region) and the Bakuriani / Borjomi area are popular astro-camping destinations. The high plateau around Abastumani Observatory is historically used by the Georgian National Astrophysical Observatory. Best months: October–April for clear, stable skies; July–September for warm summer Milky Way views.

title: When to Observe in Georgia
text: Georgia's summer (June–August) brings warm but humid nights and short darkness — sunset around 21:00, dawn around 04:00 means barely 4 hours of true dark. Autumn (September–October) is the sweet spot: long nights, moderate temperatures, generally clear. Winter (December–February) has the longest nights but cold and frequent cloud cover; the Caucasus mountains can be exceptional on clear nights. Spring is unpredictable.

title: Beginner Telescope Budgets
text: Under $200: skip telescopes; buy 10x50 binoculars and a planisphere. You'll see craters, the Pleiades, M31, and large open clusters. Best learning investment. $300–$500: 6" or 8" Dobsonian (Sky-Watcher Classic 150P or 200P). $500–$1000: 10" Dobsonian, or a quality 80mm apochromatic refractor on a tracking mount. $1500+: 12" Dobsonian with goto, or a serious astrophotography setup (mount + scope + camera). Astroman can advise based on observing goals and Georgian sky conditions.

title: Why Binoculars Before a Telescope
text: 10x50 or 7x50 binoculars are the most underrated astronomy tool. They show: craters on the Moon, the four Galilean moons of Jupiter, the Pleiades and Hyades clusters, M31 Andromeda Galaxy as a clear smudge, M13 Hercules cluster, the Milky Way as a river of stars from dark sites, comets when bright. Cheap (~$50–150 for decent quality), portable, no setup, no maintenance. Most beginners who buy a telescope first regret skipping binoculars.

title: Astronomy with Kids
text: For ages 5–10: start with the Moon (any binoculars), then Jupiter and Saturn through a borrowed telescope, then learning constellations from a planisphere. Avoid faint deep-sky targets — kids lose interest waiting in cold for a dim smudge. Best targets in order of impact: Moon at first quarter, Saturn's rings, Jupiter and moons, Pleiades, M42 Orion Nebula. For ages 10+: a tabletop Dobsonian (Heritage 130P, Astroman starter scopes) is the right starting telescope.

title: Astrophotography — Where to Start
text: Three paths: (1) Smartphone afocal — hold a phone over the eyepiece, capture the Moon and bright planets. Fastest entry. (2) DSLR + tripod — wide-field nightscapes (Milky Way arcs, star trails, meteor showers). 14–24mm lens, ISO 1600–3200, 15–25 second exposures. (3) Tracked deep-sky — equatorial mount + camera + telescope or telephoto lens. Steeper learning curve but the only way to capture galaxies and faint nebulae. Don't try (3) before mastering (2).

title: Photographing the Milky Way
text: Need: dark sky (Bortle 4 or better), wide lens (14–24mm at f/2.8 or wider), camera with manual mode, sturdy tripod. Settings: ISO 3200, f/2.8, 20–25 seconds (longer = star trails). Focus manually on a bright star — autofocus fails in the dark. Best months for Milky Way core: April–October from the northern hemisphere; June–August is peak. Phone night-mode helps but won't match a real camera.

title: Watching Meteor Showers
text: Best annual showers: Quadrantids (early January, ~120/hr peak), Lyrids (late April, ~20/hr), Eta Aquarids (early May, ~50/hr), Perseids (mid-August, ~100/hr — easiest because of warm weather), Orionids (late October, ~25/hr), Leonids (mid-November, variable, occasional storm), Geminids (mid-December, ~150/hr peak). Watch from a reclining chair, eyes naked, look 60–90° from the radiant for the longest trails. Moon phase matters more than instrument — bright moon kills meteor watching.

title: Occultations and Conjunctions
text: An occultation is when the Moon passes in front of a star or planet. The disappearance/reappearance can be timed to the second and is fascinating in any telescope. A conjunction is a close pairing of two solar-system objects. Predict events with apps like SkySafari or Stellarium. Saturn-Jupiter great conjunction (December 2020, next in 2080) was visible in one telescope field — extremely rare.

title: Double Stars Worth Seeing
text: Double stars are easy targets even in poor skies — light pollution doesn't matter much. Top picks: Albireo (Cygnus, blue+gold, 50x splits it), Mizar/Alcor (Big Dipper handle, naked-eye plus telescopic split), Cor Caroli (Canes Venatici, white+yellow), Almach (Andromeda, gold+blue), Castor (Gemini, twin white pair), the Double Double in Lyra (quadruple system, splits into two pairs at 200x), Polaris (faint companion at 100x).

title: Variable Stars — A Lifelong Hobby
text: Variable stars change brightness over hours, days, or years. Easy starter targets: Algol (Beta Persei, eclipsing binary, drops 1.3 magnitudes every 2.87 days for 10 hours — visible to the naked eye), Mira (Omicron Ceti, magnitude 3 at peak, 10 at minimum, 332-day period). The AAVSO maintains charts and accepts amateur observations that contribute to professional research.

title: What Field Mode Means
text: Field Mode in Stellar runs the AI Space Companion entirely on your phone, with no internet required. The language model and astronomy reference data are downloaded once (about 700MB) and then work forever in airplane mode. Use Field Mode at dark-sky sites where there is no signal, when conserving cellular data, or when you want responses to stay private. Quality is slightly lower than the cloud Claude model but covers everything in the on-device astronomy corpus.
